candidate_problems.text: How can we non-circularly identify the 'minimal load-bearing generator' of a phenomenon when many inequivalent generative models can fit the same observed behavior? The conjecture depends on explanation uniquely or preferentially locating the right underlying program, but it leaves open what principled criteria distinguish the genuine generator from underdetermined alternatives, especially when access to the true mechanism is indirect.

What exactly counts as a 'real constraint' in a generative explanation, and how can load-bearingness be operationalized? The conjecture contrasts explanations with redescriptions by saying explanatory details are not interchangeable, but it does not yet specify a robust test for when changing a component genuinely alters explanatory content rather than merely changing implementation details or level of description.

How should higher-level explanations be evaluated in irreducible systems when they are not minimal generators but still support intervention, control, or understanding? The conjecture risks collapsing explanation into micro-generative mechanism, leaving unresolved whether macro-level causal models, universality classes, or effective theories can be genuinely explanatory even if they do not identify the most primitive rule.
